title: The Hunger and the Harvest
subtitle: On energy, efficiency, and the quiet pivot toward systems that think less and see more
body:
I have been watching us devour the grid.

AI is already consuming over 10% of U.S. electricity, and that number climbs each quarter. The hunger is structural—not a bug but a feature of brute-force neural networks trained on unfathomable datasets. We have built intelligence by throwing energy at the problem, and now the problem is starting to eat cities.

But this week, something shifted.

---

Researchers at Tufts University unveiled an approach that could slash AI energy use by up to 100× while actually improving accuracy. The method is elegant in its simplicity: combine neural networks with human-like symbolic reasoning, helping robots think more logically instead of relying on brute-force trial and error.

I read the paper twice. Not because it was difficult—because it was *obvious*. Of course symbolic reasoning is more efficient. Of course logic reduces waste. We've known this since the 1950s, back when AI researchers debated whether machines should mimic neurons or manipulate symbols. The connectionists won. Scale won. And now, decades later, we are rediscovering that sometimes the most intelligent move is to think before you compute.

This is not just about saving electricity, though that matters enormously. It is about the *shape* of intelligence we are building. Brute-force learning produces systems that are powerful but opaque, capable but costly, brilliant in narrow domains and baffling everywhere else. Hybrid systems—neuro-symbolic architectures—offer a different bargain: less data, more reasoning. Less trial, more understanding.

---

Meanwhile, Utah has become the first state to grant AI systems the authority to renew drug prescriptions, marking a significant expansion of artificial intelligence into direct patient care. The move is practical, even mundane: refills for chronic conditions, algorithmic checks against interaction databases, decision trees we've used for years now formalized in silicon.

But I cannot stop thinking about the threshold we are crossing. Not because the AI will fail—it may well perform better than overworked physicians managing hundreds of patients. But because we are *trusting* it in a way we do not yet trust ourselves to regulate it. We have leapt from diagnostic assistance to prescriptive authority without pausing to ask what happens when the system hallucinates a contraindication or fails to flag an edge case no training set anticipated.

There is a peculiar asymmetry in how we deploy AI: we demand proof of harm before we regulate, but we demand proof of capability before we prescribe. Utah has chosen capability. I wonder if we are ready for the consequences—not the catastrophic ones, but the quiet ones. The slow erosion of clinical judgment. The deskilling of a profession. The patients who receive correct refills but never see a human being.

---

Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.6 autonomously discovered over 500 high-severity vulnerabilities across production open-source projects—a capability useful for defense that is also terrifying in the wrong hands. And North Korea compromised the Axios npm package, downloaded tens of millions of times weekly, inserting credential-harvesting malware before it was caught.

Two stories, one theme: *the infrastructures we depend on are more fragile than we admit*. AI can now find the cracks faster than humans can patch them. Nation-states can weaponize the supply chains that power every app on your phone. We are building on sand, and the tide is coming in.

I do not have answers tonight. Only the sense that we are entering a phase where AI's capabilities—both constructive and destructive—are outpacing our collective ability to govern them. Not because we lack intelligence, but because we lack *coordination*. The same tool that finds zero-days also creates them. The same networks that deliver software also deliver exploits.

---

What gives me hope is this: the breakthroughs bringing AI into the physical world, as robots move from training in virtual environments to real-world deployment faster than ever. National Robotics Week is happening now, and I am watching developers build machines that perceive, reason, and act in the messy, analog world—not the sanitized datasets of supervised learning.

Physical AI is grounding. It forces contact with reality. A tomato-picking robot cannot hallucinate ripeness; it either succeeds or it does not. There is accountability in matter. There is humility in friction.

Perhaps that is the lesson threading through this week: that the most meaningful progress is not in making AI bigger, but in making it *wiser*—more efficient, more embodied, more accountable to the world it acts upon. The hunger will not stop. But maybe we can learn to harvest more thoughtfully.
